nativeRange.habitat: Medium to large lowland streams, canals, lakes, and swamps with muddy or sandy bottoms; shallow to moderately deep slow or still water with aquatic vegetation; also enters brackish estuaries

decorPreferences: floating plants or surface vegetation for shade and cover
minimal sharp decor — gar may lunge and injure themselves
driftwood or large smooth rocks as resting points
tight-fitting lid required (obligate air-breather; strong jumper)
low to moderate flow — prefers sluggish or still water

diet.notes: In the wild, the Florida Gar feeds primarily on fish, with shrimp making up roughly 17% of the diet and smaller amounts of crayfish, frogs, and other aquatic prey. It takes prey using an extremely fast lunging strike that lasts only 25 to 40 milliseconds. Captive specimens often refuse prepared foods at first and can be difficult to transition away from live feeders. Frozen whole fish such as tilapia and smelt, large prawns, and freeze-dried krill are suitable staple foods. A diet based solely on live feeder fish is not recommended, as it increases the risk of introducing pathogens and can result in nutritional imbalance. Juveniles do best with 2 to 3 small meals offered 5 to 6 days per week; feeding frequency can be reduced for large adults.

goodWith: other large gar of similar size
large bichirs (Polypterus) of similar body size
large non-aggressive characins too large to swallow
avoidWith: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth — will be eaten
highly aggressive cichlids (Peacock Bass, Flowerhorn, Jaguar Cichlid)
fish with long trailing fins (bite/injury risk during prey strikes)

imageCandidates.notes: Raimond Spekking; 2000x1583. Sharp, well-lit planted-aquarium shot of three Lepisosteus gars clearly showing the diagnostic dark rounded spots on body and fins, ganoid-armored elongated bodies and long snouts — the most striking hobbyist-context image. Caveat: file labeled only to genus Lepisosteus; near-indistinguishable from Spotted Gar at species level.
Amaury Laporte; 6000x4000. Species-labeled Florida Gar in situ among lily pads, whole fish with olive spotting visible; dimmer low-light pond exposure so used as backup rather than lead.
Angelica Burgos-Peraza; 2048x946 (via GBIF, per-media CC BY). Research-grade L. platyrhincus at the surface showing spotted pattern and long snout; slightly soft, kept as species-confirmed alternate.

summary: The Florida Gar is a living relic of the ancient Holostei lineage, encased in tough bony scales and capable of breathing atmospheric air directly from the surface. Reaching up to 75 cm, it requires very large aquaria of at least 1000 litres and is best suited to experienced keepers of large predatory fish.
careNotes:
Lepisosteus platyrhincus, the Florida Gar, is one of the smaller gar species yet still reaches 60–75 cm in aquaria (wild maximum recorded at 132 cm). It belongs to the ancient ray-finned Holostei lineage — the same class as bowfin — making it a true 'living fossil' with a lineage stretching back over 100 million years. Its ganoid scales form a near-impenetrable bony armour unlike the cycloid or ctenoid scales of most modern teleosts.

OBLIGATE AIR-BREATHER: The Florida Gar must have unobstructed surface access at all times. A sealed or densely planted surface will cause suffocation. A tight-fitting lid with ventilation gaps is essential — this species is a powerful jumper and escape risk is high.

TANK: A minimum of 1000 L with a long footprint (240 cm+) is required for a single adult. The tank should be wide and relatively shallow rather than tall, maximising surface area and swimming lanes. Decor must be smooth and sparse; gar lunge at prey with extreme speed and can injure their long beak on sharp objects.

WATER: Broadly tolerant of water chemistry — temperature 18–28°C, pH 6.5–8.0, moderate softness to moderately hard. Excellent filtration is critical given the heavy bioload of large meaty meals. Weekly large water changes (30–50%) are strongly advised.

DIET: Weaning off live feeders reduces disease risk. Offer frozen-thawed whole fish (smelt, tilapia, whitebait), large prawns, and freeze-dried krill. Some individuals accept high-protein pellets over time. Live or fresh-killed prey can help stimulate feeding in new specimens.

CONSPECIFIC: Can be kept in groups if raised together from juveniles and all specimens are similar in size; adults kept singly are safer.

SPECIES CONFUSION: Often confused with the Spotted Gar (L. oculatus) — distinguished by its broader, shorter snout and lack of spots on the underside of the jaw. The Alligator Gar (Atractosteus spatula) is a far larger and entirely distinct species reaching 3 m+, and is not suitable for any home aquarium.

LEGAL: Many gar species are subject to exotic/invasive species regulations. Possession or transport of live gars is restricted or prohibited in several US Midwestern states including Illinois and Iowa. Always verify local, state, and federal regulations before acquiring this species.

LONG-LIVED: Expect a commitment of 15–26+ years. Wild fish have been recorded to at least 20 years; captive specimens can exceed this with excellent care.
breedingNotes: Breeding in home aquaria is extremely rare — only a handful of documented captive successes exist, almost all in large outdoor ponds or commercial facilities. Females (sexually mature at 3–4 years) deposit adhesive greenish eggs over vegetation; multiple males fertilise externally. No parental care is provided after spawning. Eggs and yolk-sac fry are toxic to predators due to ichthyotoxic compounds. Fry initially consume zooplankton, transitioning to small live fish as they grow. A very large, well-planted pond-scale setup with multiple individuals is required to attempt captive breeding.

primary.name: FishBase — Lepisosteus platyrhincus
primary.notes: Primary biological reference. Provided taxonomy (Lepisosteidae, Lepisosteiformes, Holostei), maximum size (132 cm TL, common length ~59.5 cm), native range (Savannah River drainage, GA to Ocklockonee River drainage, FL; peninsular Florida), habitat (sluggish mud/sand-bottomed pools near vegetation), facultative air-breather status, trophic level 3.7 (carnivore), reproductive biology, and low population resilience (minimum doubling time 4.5–14 years).

additional.name: MonsterFishKeepers.com — Florida Gar Care Sheet
additional.notes: Specialist large-fish keeper community care sheet. Provided temperature range 50–85°F (optimal 76–78°F), pH 6.5–8.0 (optimal 7.0–7.5), minimum tank 200 gal (757 L) for single adult, diet (piscivore; can accept freeze-dried krill, prawns, tilapia fillet), swim zone (primarily mid-water to surface for air), breeding rarity in captivity (~2 documented home aquarium successes), and medication sensitivity notes.

additional.name: AquariumDomain — Florida Gar Species Profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist species profile. Confirmed temperature 68–84°F, pH 6.5–7.8, KH 8–20, minimum 180 gal for adults (300–450 gal ideal), carnivore diet (fish, prawns, shrimp, crickets), semi-aggressive temperament toward smaller fish, sand/gravel substrate, spawning season April–May in shallow vegetated areas.

additional.name: Animal Diversity Web — Lepisosteus platyrhincus
additional.notes: Academic biological reference (University of Michigan). Confirmed max size 132.2 cm, native range (Savannah River GA to Ocklockonee River FL and peninsular Florida), depth range 0.6–5.2 m, diet (primarily fish, 17% shrimp, crayfish), ram-feeding strike technique (25–40 ms), spawning February–April, female maturity 3–4 years, male maturity 2 years, social grouping of 2–10+ individuals, and aestivation behaviour.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity.com — Florida Gar
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist care reference. Confirmed temperature 24–27°C, pH 7.0–7.5, 250-gallon/1000 L minimum for multiple specimens, piscivore diet (live or frozen fish preferred), preference for low-current environments with floating plants and minimal sharp objects.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding in home aquaria is extremely rare, with only around two documented successes on record. A large outdoor pond-scale setup, multiple conspecifics, and seasonal temperature cues appear to be necessary conditions. The eggs are ichthyotoxic — toxic to predators — and fry begin by feeding on zooplankton before transitioning to small live fish.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Florida Gar reaches ~75 cm; requires 1000 L+ tanks with surface area for air-breathing. Aggressive piscivore. Specialist large-fish setup. Note: many gar species are LEGALLY-RESTRICTED in US Midwestern states under Illinois/Iowa exotic species rules — verify local regulations before keeping.
